skill.name: skill-market-publisher
skill.description: Publish, submit, and verify local AI skills across public skill marketplaces, directories, and registries. The skill prepares metadata, review bundles, live submissions, manual submission notes, and post-submit verification steps.
skill.summary: Prepare, publish, and verify AI skill listings across multiple public marketplaces.
skill.author: zjsxply

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, network, filesystem, and blocker patterns. Review found no prompt injection attempt and no confirmed malicious intent; most markdown backtick and weak-crypto hits are false positives from documentation and literal marketplace examples. The skill still has medium risk because it intentionally reads local skill files, writes bundles, opens URLs, runs limited subprocess commands, and can submit skill metadata or full SKILL.md content to third-party marketplaces when the operator uses --execute.

medium_findings.title: Live Third-Party Marketplace Submission
medium_findings.description: The publishing script can send repository URLs, author email, seller wallet fields, and full SKILL.md content to hardcoded marketplace endpoints. This matches the skill purpose and live submission requires an explicit execute flag, but it can disclose skill content and contact details to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly builds external payloads and posts them to marketplace URLs. The risk is intentional and gated by --execute, so it is not evidence of malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Subprocess Execution For Git, Browser, And CLI Publishing
medium_findings.description: The scripts invoke git, the local open command, and a configurable ClawHub executable. Calls use argument lists rather than shell strings, which reduces injection risk, but running local executables remains a meaningful operator-trust boundary.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: subprocess.run is directly present at the cited locations. The no-shell argument-list usage and explicit CLI workflow make command injection less likely, but execution is real.

medium_findings.title: Local Skill File Reads And Bundle Writes
medium_findings.description: The CLI reads a user-selected local SKILL.md and writes manifest, plan, notes, and payload files under a user-selected output directory. This is expected behavior, but users should not run it on private skills or unsafe output paths without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file reads and writes are explicit and central to bundle generation. No hidden file harvesting was found, but local file access is still part of the runtime surface.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many Ruby or shell backtick findings occur in markdown code spans that name commands, modes, URLs, and response fields. These are documentation examples, not executable Ruby code in the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are markdown and the flagged text is inside prose or fenced command examples. There is no Ruby interpreter path for these snippets.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography blocker hits align with words such as hash, status, or marketplace fields rather than cryptographic API calls. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or custom cryptography used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited contexts found frontmatter parsing, prose, or ordinary fields. I did not find cryptographic library use at those locations.

low_findings.title: Hidden File And Sensitive Key Detections Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The hidden file finding points to a documented GitHub workflow URL, and the certificate or key-file finding points to market list handling. No evidence found that the skill reads SSH keys, certificates, tokens, or hidden credential files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited markdown URL contains .github as repository path text, and line 1501 selects market names. Neither location accesses local credentials or key material.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Patterns Are Bounded Marketplace Checks
low_findings.description: Recon commands and HTTP fetches are used to confirm public marketplace pages and expected markers. This is legitimate for a publishing tool, but users should expect outbound requests during recon.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The recon behavior is explicit and limited to configured marketplace URLs. It is not stealthy, but it is real outbound network activity.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP POST To External Marketplaces
dangerous_patterns.description: post_json and post_form send generated payloads to third-party marketplace endpoints during live publish operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network post helpers and submit functions are directly visible. They are expected for this publishing skill but still need operator consent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Configurable Local Executable Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The ClawHub path can be supplied by the operator and is executed with subprocess.run during CLI publishing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The executable name is configurable and then executed as a subprocess. Argument-list invocation limits shell injection but does not remove executable trust risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Full Skill Content Included In Some Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: Bogen.ai and skillsrep payload builders include read_skill_markdown output, which can expose the entire selected SKILL.md to those services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The payload builders directly call read_skill_markdown for external submission fields. This is clear content disclosure when users publish to those markets.

content.value_statement: Publishing one AI skill across many marketplaces requires different payloads, URLs, and verification steps. This skill builds release bundles, runs supported submissions, and guides manual marketplace workflows.
actual_capabilities: Inspects a local skill folder and extracts SKILL.md metadata.
Classifies repository layout for single-skill, pack, and nested skill repositories.
Builds marketplace readiness plans with missing fields and layout warnings.
Creates submission bundles with manifests, payloads, and manual submission notes.
Runs verified live publishing adapters only when the execute flag is provided.
Checks known marketplace pages for expected submission markers during recon.
limitations: Marketplace behavior can change, so recon should run before live publishing.
Some targets still require manual login, seller onboarding, or browser submission.
Live submissions can send skill content and contact fields to third-party services.
The tool does not guarantee public listing approval after a successful submit response.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Marketplace Release
use_cases.description: Create a readiness plan and bundle before submitting a new skill to public directories.

use_cases.title: Verify Existing Listings
use_cases.description: Check public status pages, search endpoints, and listing URLs after marketplace submission.

use_cases.title: Maintain Publishing Adapters
use_cases.description: Re-run recon and update market notes when a marketplace changes its submission surface.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect My Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check before any marketplace work.
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect this local skill folder and summarize its metadata, repository layout, and likely marketplace readiness.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Release Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a marketplace readiness plan for this skill using my repository URL, branch, category, version, and contact fields.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Review Bundle
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a submission bundle for this skill and highlight which markets are ready, missing inputs, or manual only.

prompt_templates.title: Publish and Verify
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the dry-run publish flow for one verified market, review the payload, then perform live submission only after I approve execution.

output_examples.input: A local skill folder plus a GitHub repository URL and branch name.
output: A readiness matrix showing supported markets, missing fields, and layout warnings.
A bundle containing marketplace payloads and manual submission instructions.
A verification checklist for each submitted or pending marketplace.

output_examples.input: A request to publish one skill to a verified anonymous marketplace.
output: A dry-run payload preview for operator review.
A live submission result only after explicit execution.
Next verification targets such as status pages, search APIs, or public listing URLs.

output_examples.input: A marketplace that may have changed its submission page.
output: A recon result that reports matched and missing page markers.
A recommendation to keep, downgrade, or manually review the adapter.
Notes about authentication, layout scope, and reliable public verification signals.

best_practices: Run inspect and plan before creating bundles or live submissions.
Review generated payloads before using the execute flag for any market.
Verify public listing state with the strongest available market-specific signal.
anti_patterns: Do not submit private skills or unpublished contact details to public marketplaces.
Do not treat a successful HTTP response as proof of a public listing.
Do not automate login-gated or unstable endpoints without a reviewed adapter update.

faq.question: Does this skill publish automatically by default?
faq.answer: No. Live publishing requires an explicit execute flag for supported adapters.

faq.question: Can it handle manual marketplace flows?
faq.answer: Yes. It opens tracked submit pages and prints concise operator checklists for manual targets.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code skills?
faq.answer: Yes. It works with SKILL.md based skills and declares support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What information can be sent externally?
faq.answer: Depending on the market, it may send repository URLs, skill metadata, author email, wallet fields, or SKILL.md content.

faq.question: Does a successful submit mean the skill is listed?
faq.answer: No. Many marketplaces use queues or review steps, so public verification is still required.

faq.question: When should recon be used?
faq.answer: Use recon before changing adapters or making current support claims about a marketplace.
